title: Quantum optical photoelectron interferometry
authors: Jonathan Dubois, Viviane Cotte, Richard Taïeb, Camille Lévêque, Jérémie Caillat, Pranshu Dave, Pascal Salières, David Bresteau, Charles Bourassin-Bouchet, Anne L'Huillier, David Busto
abstract: We present a general theoretical framework for multiphoton processes driven by quantum light fields, establishing a direct link between photon statistics and photoelectron observables. Our results show that the autocorrelation and cross-correlation functions, which quantify the underlying photon statistics, are directly mapped onto the resulting photoelectron spectra. Although our framework is broadly applicable, we demonstrate specifically in the example of reconstruction of attosecond beating by interference of two-photon transitions (RABBIT) the influence of the light statistical properties. In this approach, the amplitude, contrast and phase of the oscillations of the sideband signal as a function of pump-probe delay reveal the quantum nature of light. We analyze these observables across several quantum configurations, including correlated infrared and harmonic modes, as well as the uncorrelated case with non-classical harmonic statistics, thereby establishing a general framework for quantum-light RABBIT spectroscopy. We compare the analytical theory with numerical simulations for the case of classical harmonics and an infrared field in a squeezed coherent state, obtaining excellent agreement. Our results reveal how the interplay between classical and quantum correlations dictates the coherence of the photoemission process, providing a new window into the quantum-optical foundations of attosecond science.
summary: The paper develops a comprehensive quantum theoretical framework to analyze photoelectron interferometry using RABBIT spectroscopy. It demonstrates that the measured spectral features (amplitude, contrast, and phase) are not merely determined by the mean intensity but directly map onto higher-order correlation functions of the driving light field. This provides a powerful tool for probing the non-classical nature of intense laser sources.
main_problem: To establish a general theoretical framework linking photon statistics (quantum light fields) to photoelectron observables in multiphoton processes, specifically focusing on quantum-light RABBIT spectroscopy.
main_result: The amplitude, contrast, and phase of sideband oscillations in the photoemission spectrum directly encode the underlying quantum correlations and non-classical nature of the driving laser light field.
method: The analysis employs Time-Dependent Perturbation Theory (TDPT) applied to the density matrix evolution governed by the interaction Hamiltonian in the dipole approximation. It compares results derived from full quantum treatments against semiclassical approximations.
model_or_system: The system involves photoelectron interferometry driven by intense laser fields, utilizing High-Order Harmonic Generation (HHG) and the RABBIT technique. The light field can be modeled using various quantum states, including squeezed coherent states.
key_observables: Autocorrelation and cross-correlation functions of photon statistics; sideband oscillation amplitude ($A$), contrast ($C$), and phase ($	heta$) in the photoelectron spectrum $I(E)$.
important_parameters: Squeezing parameter ($\zeta$ or $r$), squeezing phase ($\varphi$), infrared photon number ($n_0$), and inter-mode coherence measures ($g^{(1)}_{ea}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the dipole approximation and length gauge, and often assumes photoemission occurs in a single channel.
figures_summary: Figures compare intensity spectra from TDPT versus Time-Dependent Schrödinger Equation (TDSE) simulations across various squeezing parameters ($\epsilon_0$) and phases ($\varphi$), illustrating how quantum fluctuations modify the RABBIT signal.
paper_structure: The paper develops a general formalism using density matrices, applies it to the RABBIT scheme by calculating sideband intensities via TDPT, and then investigates specific quantum regimes (squeezed states) by comparing theoretical predictions with numerical simulations.
why_interesting: This work provides a rigorous quantum-optical foundation for attosecond science, showing how subtle quantum correlations in the driving laser field manifest as measurable signatures in photoelectron spectra, extending beyond simple classical phase assumptions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) The Wigner distribution W(α) of a squeezed coherent state of light [see Eq. (48a)], represented at suc- cessive instants over one optical cycle of approximately 2.7 fs (laser wavelength of 800 nm), as a function of the real and imaginary parts of α. It displays the geometry of the coherent part characterized by α0 and the squeezing ampli- tude ζ = reiφ. The coherent-state limit corresponds to ζ = 0. We use 10 photons. The coherent part is ∣α0∣= 3.15 and arg α0 = 0.4 rad. The squeezing ratio is r ∼0.37 with a phase φ = 2.37 rad. FIG. 2. (a) Illustration of the RABBIT scheme in the regime of quantum light. Two types of quantum paths contribute to the photoelectron spectrum: one-photon absorption, giv- ing rise to main bands (e.g. at frequencies ωe and ωa, shown in blue and purple, respectively), and two-photon transitions involving an additional photon of frequency ω0, giving rise to sidebands (e.g. the one shown in red between the two highlighted harmonic bands). (b) Photoelectron intensity spectrum obtained from the solution of the TDSE (see Ap- pendix C for details) as a function of τ and the energy of the photoelectron at the detector E, with τ the delay between the harmonic and infrared pulses. Other harmonics...

FIG. 4. Comparison of the (a) amplitude, (b) contrast and (c) phase of the RABBIT signal as a function of the sideband energy. The solid lines are obtained from the TDPT [Eq. (23)] and the colored circles from the TDSE. The parameters are the same as in Fig. 3, i.e. r ∼19, the number of photons is n0 = 1016 and arg α0 = 0. Note that in (a) the solid curves were scaled by 0.7729, as in Fig. 3. In (c) the phase is not displayed for ζ = r since the contrast is zero.

FIG. 3. Comparison of the intensity spectrum obtained from the TDPT (solid lines) [Eq. (23)] and the TDSE (colored cir- cles). Note that all the solid curves are scaled by 0.7729 in order to take into account the finite-pulse duration. The num- ber of photons is n0 = 1016 and arg α0 = 0. The different sub- plots correspond to different squeezing amplitudes: ε0 = 0 in (a), corresponding to a coherent state with ζ = 0, and ε0 = 1/2 in subsequent cases (corresponding to r ∼19). (b) φ = π cor- responding to a phase-squeezed state (ζ = −r), (c) φ = π/2 corresponding to an intermediate phase (ζ = i r), and (d) φ = 0 corresponding to an amplitude-squeezed state (ζ = r).

Figure 7 presents the RABBIT sidebands obtained us- ing TDPT (solid lines) and TDSE (colored circles), analo- gous to Fig. 3, but for a higher infrared photon occupancy of n0 = 1018. At this photon number, the TDPT and TDSE results no longer exhibit perfect overlap. In par- ticular, the discrepancy is more pronounced for squeezed states (panels b–c) than for the coherent state (panel a). This deviation arises because the Wigner distributions for squeezed states span several orders of magnitude in the effective field intensity.
